# **Dataset Review: Turing Multi-Speaker Multilingual ASR Corpus — Deliveries 07/01/2026 & 07/07/2026**

**Source guidelines:** `transcription_and_qa_guidelines.md`, `guidelines_for_languages/` (per-language transcription specifications)

**Evidence reviewed:** [Batch 4 automated QA](http://batch4_5_qa/qa_output_batch4/qa_report_batch4.md), [Batch 5 automated QA](http://batch4_5_qa/qa_output_batch5/qa_report_batch5.md), [DNSMOS P.835 results](http://dnsmos_batch1_5/reports/dnsmos_stats_batch1_5.md), and [effective-bandwidth analysis](http://bw_enery_analysis/reports/bandwidth_analysis_batch1_5.md).

**Sessions reviewed:** 96 channel-separated, multi-speaker conversational sessions containing **561 individual speaker channels** and **96 mixed recordings** across 10 languages: **Delivery Batch 4 (07/01/2026): 43 sessions / 227 channels** and **Delivery Batch 5 (07/07/2026): 53 sessions / 334 channels**.

| Language | Sessions | Count |
| :---- | :---- | :---: |
| Arabic (ar) | `NV-AR-SS10-CONVO24`, `NV-AR-SS10-CONVO25`, `NV-AR-SS11-CONVO26`, `NV-AR-SS11-CONVO27`, `NV-AR-SS11-CONVO28`, `NV-AR-SS12-CONVO29`, `NV-AR-SS12-CONVO30`, `NV-AR-SS13-CONVO32`, `NV-AR-SS13-CONVO33`, `NV-AR-SS13-CONVO34` | 10 |
| German (de) | `NV-GR-SS08-CONVO15`, `NV-GR-SS08-CONVO16`, `NV-GR-SS09-CONVO18`, `NV-GR-SS10-CONVO19`, `NV-GR-SS09-CONVO17`, `NV-GR-SS13-CONVO22`, `NV-GR-SS13-CONVO23` | 7 |
| English (en) | `NV-EN-SS09-CONVO22`, `NV-EN-SS12-CONVO30`, `NV-EN-SS12-CONVO31`, `NV-EN-SS12-CONVO32`, `NV-EN-SS15-CONVO37`, `NV-EN-SS13-CONVO33`, `NV-EN-SS14-CONVO35`, `NV-EN-SS14-CONVO36`, `NV-EN-SS15-CONVO38`, `NV-EN-SS16-CONVO39`, `NV-EN-SS16-CONVO40` | 11 |
| Spanish (es) | `NV-ES-SS09-CONVO20`, `NV-ES-SS09-CONVO21`, `NV-ES-SS09-CONVO22`, `NV-ES-SS10-CONVO23`, `NV-ES-SS10-CONVO24`, `NV-ES-SS11-CONVO25`, `NV-ES-SS11-CONVO26`, `NV-ES-SS12-CONVO27`, `NV-ES-SS12-CONVO28`, `NV-ES-SS13-CONVO29`, `NV-ES-SS13-CONVO30`, `NV-ES-SS14-CONVO33`, `NV-ES-SS15-CONVO16`, `NV-ES-SS15-CONVO34`, `NV-ES-SS16-CONVO31`, `NV-ES-SS16-CONVO32` | 16 |
| French (fr) | `NV-FR-SS16-CONVO17`, `NV-FR-SS17-CONVO18`, `NV-FR-SS18-CONVO20`, `NV-FR-SS18-CONVO19`, `NV-FR-SS19-CONVO23`, `NV-FR-SS20-CONVO21`, `NV-FR-SS21-CONVO25`, `NV-FR-SS24-CONVO29` | 8 |
| Italian (it) | `NV-IT-SS09-CONVO25`, `NV-IT-SS09-CONVO26`, `NV-IT-SS10-CONVO27`, `NV-IT-SS11-CONVO28`, `NV-IT-SS11-CONVO29`, `NV-IT-SS12-CONVO30`, `NV-IT-SS12-CONVO32`, `NV-IT-SS13-CONVO33`, `NV-IT-SS13-CONVO34`, `NV-IT-SS14-CONVO35` | 10 |
| Japanese (ja) | `NV-JA-SS06-CONVO18`, `NV-JA-SS07-CONVO19`, `NV-JA-SS07-CONVO21`, `NV-JA-SS08-CONVO23`, `NV-JA-SS10-CONVO29` | 5 |
| Korean (ko) | `NV-KO-SS07-CONVO18`, `NV-KO-SS07-CONVO19`, `NV-KO-SS08-CONVO20`, `NV-KO-SS08-CONVO21`, `NV-KO-SS09-CONVO23`, `NV-KO-SS09-CONVO24`, `NV-KO-SS07-CONVO17`, `NV-KO-SS08-CONVO22`, `NV-KO-SS10-CONVO25`, `NV-KO-SS11-CONVO26`, `NV-KO-SS11-CONVO27`, `NV-KO-SS11-CONVO28` | 12 |
| Portuguese (pt) | `NV-PT-SS08-CONVO20`, `NV-PT-SS08-CONVO22`, `NV-PT-SS09-CONVO24`, `NV-PT-SS10-CONVO25`, `NV-PT-SS08-CONVO23`, `NV-PT-SS10-CONVO26`, `NV-PT-SS11-CONVO28`, `NV-PT-SS12-CONVO30`, `NV-PT-SS13-CONVO32` | 9 |
| Russian (ru) | `NV-RU-SS03-CONVO16`, `NV-RU-SS03-CONVO17`, `NV-RU-SS04-CONVO22`, `NV-RU-SS05-CONVO09`, `NV-RU-SS03-CONVO28`, `NV-RU-SS04-CONVO23`, `NV-RU-SS05-CONVO27`, `NV-RU-SS06-CONVO10` | 8 |
| **Total** | Batch 4: 43 · Batch 5: 53 | **96** |

> Note: the `GR` session tag denotes **German** (`de`) content, not Greek.

All automated QA metrics were computed over annotated single-channel speech segments. DNSMOS and bandwidth statistics in the main analysis use the original WAV files; silence outside annotated speech is excluded from the quality windows.

---

## **1\. Executive Summary**

### **1.1 Scoring scale**

Each completed section is rated on a 1–5 scale. A value of **−1** means that the section is deliberately deferred pending amended source audio; it is not a negative quality score.

| Score | Quality level | Status label | Definition |
| :---: | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **5** | Excellent | Accept as-is | Fully meets the reviewed requirements; no material correction is needed. |
| **4** | Good | Accept with minor edits | Mostly compliant, with isolated and readily correctable issues. |
| **3** | Acceptable | Revision required | Recurrent but bounded issues require a documented correction pass. |
| **2** | Poor | Major revision required | Systemic issues affect a substantial portion of the delivery. |
| **1** | Unacceptable | Reject / re-deliver | Critical source-quality failures make the affected data unsuitable as delivered. |
| **−1** | Deferred | Re-evaluate | A reliable decision depends on corrected upstream data that has not yet been delivered. |

**Acceptance threshold:** every scored section must be at least 4\. Any section scored 1–2 blocks acceptance; a deferred section must be re-evaluated before the delivery can be closed.

### **1.2 Section scores**

| Section | Status | Quality score | Headline finding |
| :---- | :---- | :---: | :---- |
| 1\. Audio Quality | Reject / source amendment required | **1** | Significant quality defects were confirmed. Several channels are effectively bandwidth-limited near 8 kHz, while codec, post-processing, clipping, noise-gating, and muffling artifacts create rejection-level failures. |
| 2\. Conversation Structure Quality | Accept as-is | **5** | All 96 sessions pass the applicable speaker-overlap requirement; no material conversation-structure issue was found. |
| 3\. Transcription Quality | Deferred pending amended audio | **−1** | The current ASR measurements are retained as diagnostics, but transcription quality must be re-evaluated after corrected audio is supplied. |

**Overall sample assessment: 1 — Reject as delivered; amended source audio is required.** Conversation structure is acceptable, but the confirmed audio defects block acceptance. Transcription review remains open until clean replacement recordings are available.

---

## **2\. Conversation Structure Quality**

**Score: 5 — Accept as-is.**

The minimum-overlap requirement scales with the number of speakers: 2–3 speakers \> 5%, 4–5 speakers \> 10%, and 6 or more speakers \> 12%. All 96 sessions exceed their applicable floor.

| Delivery | Sessions | Segments | Session overlap range | Aggregate overlap | Passing sessions |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| Batch 4 (07/01) | 43 | 37,114 | 14.05%–54.62% | 28.56% | 43/43 |
| Batch 5 (07/07) | 53 | 46,902 | 17.02%–65.14% | 31.66% | 53/53 |
| **Combined** | **96** | **84,016** | **14.05%–65.14%** | **30.33%** | **96/96** |

The reviewed material contains approximately **37.44 hours of session audio** and **33.90 hours of union speech**. Speaker participation, turn-taking, and overlap are consistent with the multi-speaker conversational target. No trimming or re-recording is requested for conversation structure.

If any audio is replaced, the replacement waveform, `seglst.json`, and RTTM must remain sample-accurate and mutually synchronized. This is a data-integrity requirement, not a conversation-structure correction.

---

## **3\. Audio Quality**

**Score: 1 — Reject / source amendment required.**

Basic SNR and silence-floor checks pass, but they do not measure bandwidth loss, clipping, codec smearing, aggressive noise gating, or perceptual muffling. DNSMOS, spectral analysis, and listening review identify critical defects that the basic signal checks alone do not capture.

### **3.1 Regular automated audio QA**

The regular QA thresholds are per-channel DetER ≤ 10%, mixed DetER ≤ 7%, SNR ≥ 20 dB, and silence-floor RMS ≤ −40 dB.

| Delivery | Speaker channels | SNR failures | Silence-floor failures | Channel DetER failures | Mixed DetER failures |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| Batch 4 | 227 | 0 | 0 | 15 | 7 |
| Batch 5 | 334 | 0 | 0 | 30 | 16 |
| **Combined** | **561** | **0** | **0** | **45** | **23** |

Across both batches, per-channel SNR ranges from **21.63 to 82.58 dB**, and silence-floor RMS ranges from approximately **−110.00 to −47.13 dB**. All 561 speaker channels pass both signal-level thresholds. The DetER exceedances are retained as VAD/alignment diagnostics; they are not used alone to determine perceptual audio acceptance.

### **3.2 DNSMOS P.835 results**

DNSMOS P.835 reports three calibrated scores: **SIG** (speech-signal quality), **BAK** (background-noise quality), and **OVRL** (overall quality). Scores are on a nominal 1–5 MOS-like scale; higher is better. Statistics below cover only Batch 4 and Batch 5\.

| Audio type | N | Metric | Min | Min record | Max | Max record | Average | Median |
| :---- | ----: | :---- | ----: | :---- | ----: | :---- | ----: | ----: |
| Individual | 561 | SIG | 2.707 | NV-GR-SS08-CONVO15/SPK05 | 3.699 | NV-AR-SS13-CONVO33/SPK07 | 3.365 | 3.384 |
| Individual | 561 | BAK | 3.140 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO28/SPK08 | 4.196 | NV-AR-SS12-CONVO29/SPK08 | 3.956 | 3.995 |
| Individual | 561 | OVRL | 2.189 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO28/SPK08 | 3.480 | NV-AR-SS13-CONVO33/SPK07 | 3.027 | 3.053 |
| Mixed | 96 | SIG | 3.214 | NV-RU-SS05-CONVO09/MIXED | 3.565 | NV-KO-SS07-CONVO19/MIXED | 3.433 | 3.446 |
| Mixed | 96 | BAK | 2.415 | NV-RU-SS05-CONVO27/MIXED | 3.866 | NV-FR-SS19-CONVO23/MIXED | 3.340 | 3.361 |
| Mixed | 96 | OVRL | 2.256 | NV-RU-SS05-CONVO09/MIXED | 3.132 | NV-EN-SS14-CONVO36/MIXED | 2.813 | 2.837 |

**29 of 561 individual channels (5.2%) have SIG below 3.0.** Batch 4 contains 11 such channels and Batch 5 contains 18\. The mixed recordings have higher SIG scores because stronger or wider-band speakers can mask a defective individual channel; individual-channel scores therefore govern this review.

A SIG threshold near 3.0 is useful for automated screening, but it is not sufficient as a standalone acceptance rule. Several manually rejected samples score slightly above 3.0 because DNSMOS does not fully capture every clipping, popping, bandwidth, or post-processing artifact.

### **3.3 Effective-bandwidth analysis**

The 48 kHz container rate does not guarantee useful speech energy through the 24 kHz Nyquist limit. A sustained cutoff at or below 8 kHz is consistent with an upsampled 16 kHz source or an equivalent codec/device low-pass. Spectral analysis cannot uniquely identify which upstream process caused the cutoff, so the report uses the neutral term **effective bandwidth**.

| Delivery | Channels | ≤8 kHz | ≤12 kHz | ≤12 kHz rate | ≤16 kHz | ≤16 kHz rate |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| Batch 4 | 227 | 27 | 50 | 22.0% | 211 | 93.0% |
| Batch 5 | 334 | 43 | 65 | 19.5% | 310 | 92.8% |
| **Combined** | **561** | **70** | **115** | **20.5%** | **521** | **92.9%** |

The severe ≤8 kHz group contains 70 channels (12.5%). This confirms that a meaningful portion of the nominally 48 kHz delivery has substantially narrower effective bandwidth. Mixed tracks understate the problem because high-frequency energy from one participant can conceal another participant's cutoff.

### **3.4 Listening-review findings**

The listening review identified the following rejection-level patterns in Batch 4/5:

- severe noise-gating or codec/post-processing artifacts that leave speech muffled or unnaturally isolated;  
- effective bandwidth limited near 7–8 kHz, frequently accompanied by muffling;  
- audible clipping; and  
- frequent popping artifacts on an individual speaker channel.

Thirteen Batch 4/5 channels, each from a different session, are rejected. Some non-rejected low-SIG channels contain audible reverberation, low level, background noise, or mild filtering, but were judged usable after listening.

### **3.5 Corpus-wide Worst-100 individual channels by DNSMOS SIG**

The following ranking is the existing corpus-wide review across Delivery Batches 1–5. It is included in full for auditability. **75 of the 100 channels are from Batch 4 or Batch 5\.** Review comments have been rewritten for clarity without changing their meaning or verdict.

| Rank | Batch | Session | Channel | SIG | BAK | OVRL | Speech min | Peak dBFS | Verdict | Review comments |
| ----: | :---- | :---- | :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | Batch4 | NV-GR-SS08-CONVO15 | SPK05 | 2.707 | 3.995 | 2.388 | 3.22 | \-15.10 | reject | Severe noise-gating artifact with muffled speech. |
| 2 | Batch4 | NV-GR-SS10-CONVO19 | SPK05 | 2.719 | 4.014 | 2.409 | 3.58 | \-10.28 | accept | Muffled speech at an extremely low level. |
| 3 | Batch5 | NV-PT-SS12-CONVO30 | SPK05 | 2.719 | 3.867 | 2.335 | 4.72 | \-10.59 | accept | Muffled speech with effective bandwidth limited to approximately 8 kHz. |
| 4 | Batch4 | NV-EN-SS12-CONVO30 | SPK05 | 2.743 | 3.950 | 2.456 | 3.89 | 0.00 | reject | Severe noise-gating artifact with muffled speech. |
| 5 | Batch5 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO28 | SPK08 | 2.776 | 3.140 | 2.189 | 5.29 | \-0.79 | accept | Noticeable reverberation. |
| 6 | Batch4 | NV-GR-SS08-CONVO16 | SPK04 | 2.797 | 3.761 | 2.495 | 4.80 | 0.00 | reject | Audible clipping at a rejection-level severity. |
| 7 | Batch2 | NV-JA-SS06-CONVO16 | SPK02 | 2.814 | 3.766 | 2.506 | 6.18 | 0.00 | accept | Aggressive noise gating audibly damages the speech. |
| 8 | Batch5 | NV-GR-SS09-CONVO17 | SPK07 | 2.827 | 3.827 | 2.526 | 2.25 | \-1.74 | reject | Effective bandwidth is limited to approximately 8 kHz. |
| 9 | Batch5 | NV-KO-SS11-CONVO28 | SPK06 | 2.834 | 3.247 | 2.241 | 6.55 | \-1.00 | accept | Reverberation and background noise are present, but remain acceptable. |
| 10 | Batch5 | NV-IT-SS12-CONVO30 | SPK04 | 2.846 | 3.596 | 2.413 | 5.50 | \-7.13 | reject | Effective bandwidth is limited to approximately 8 kHz. |
| 11 | Batch1 | NV-JA-SS04-CONVO11 | SPK04 | 2.854 | 3.881 | 2.576 | 6.14 | \-0.00 | accept | Aggressive noise gating produces muffled, isolated speech fragments. |
| 12 | Batch5 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO28 | SPK04 | 2.868 | 3.314 | 2.349 | 4.34 | \-0.27 | accept | Reverberation is present but remains acceptable. |
| 13 | Batch5 | NV-KO-SS11-CONVO26 | SPK06 | 2.873 | 3.719 | 2.420 | 6.63 | \-11.14 | accept | No rejection-level issue identified. |
| 14 | Batch5 | NV-ES-SS13-CONVO30 | SPK07 | 2.881 | 3.619 | 2.426 | 3.05 | \-6.63 | accept | Muffled speech with audible codec artifacts. |
| 15 | Batch5 | NV-KO-SS11-CONVO27 | SPK06 | 2.893 | 3.354 | 2.311 | 6.33 | \-8.10 | accept | No rejection-level issue identified. |
| 16 | Batch5 | NV-PT-SS12-CONVO30 | SPK02 | 2.893 | 3.259 | 2.351 | 7.31 | \-4.66 | accept | Strong reverberation is present. |
| 17 | Batch4 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO17 | SPK06 | 2.897 | 3.841 | 2.578 | 5.64 | \-4.87 | accept | Muffled speech with codec-related artifacts. |
| 18 | Batch5 | NV-ES-SS15-CONVO16 | SPK05 | 2.905 | 3.908 | 2.601 | 2.93 | \-3.12 | accept | Some low-pass filtering is audible, but the sample remains acceptable. |
| 19 | Batch4 | NV-GR-SS08-CONVO16 | SPK02 | 2.908 | 3.919 | 2.649 | 4.56 | \-2.27 | accept | No rejection-level issue identified. |
| 20 | Batch4 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO16 | SPK06 | 2.911 | 3.870 | 2.606 | 3.89 | \-4.45 | reject | Severe codec degradation and muffling, with an effective cutoff near 7 kHz. |

| 76 | Batch2 | NV-JA-SS04-CONVO10 | SPK04 | 3.093 | 3.905 | 2.815 | 5.16 | \-2.00 | accept | — |

| 78 | Batch5 | NV-AR-SS13-CONVO34 | SPK03 | 3.097 | 3.798 | 2.718 | 6.02 | \-3.98 | accept | — |
| 79 | Batch5 | NV-JA-SS08-CONVO23 | SPK05 | 3.097 | 4.012 | 2.810 | 3.88 | \-1.19 | accept | — |
| 80 | Batch2 | NV-JA-SS04-CONVO12 | SPK02 | 3.100 | 3.914 | 2.799 | 3.97 | 0.00 | accept | — |
| 81 | Batch5 | NV-ES-SS16-CONVO31 | SPK07 | 3.101 | 3.845 | 2.708 | 4.14 | \-7.55 | accept | — |
| 82 | Batch4 | NV-RU-SS04-CONVO22 | SPK04 | 3.101 | 3.917 | 2.777 | 4.27 | \-5.89 | accept | — |
| 83 | Batch2 | NV-AR-SS08-CONVO20 | SPK01 | 3.106 | 3.971 | 2.824 | 4.23 | 0.00 | accept | — |
| 84 | Batch3 | NV-ES-SS06-CONVO15 | SPK02 | 3.106 | 3.694 | 2.672 | 5.03 | \-6.07 | accept | — |
| 85 | Batch3 | NV-GR-SS07-CONVO13 | SPK04 | 3.109 | 3.911 | 2.735 | 3.07 | \-9.17 | accept | — |
| 86 | Batch2 | NV-AR-SS07-CONVO18 | SPK01 | 3.109 | 4.003 | 2.753 | 4.22 | \-7.33 | accept | — |
| 87 | Batch5 | NV-GR-SS13-CONVO23 | SPK04 | 3.111 | 3.862 | 2.738 | 3.74 | \-7.97 | accept | — |
| 88 | Batch4 | NV-ES-SS10-CONVO24 | SPK05 | 3.111 | 3.733 | 2.669 | 3.64 | \-9.12 | accept | — |
| 89 | Batch4 | NV-PT-SS08-CONVO22 | SPK02 | 3.111 | 3.869 | 2.781 | 5.22 | 0.00 | accept | — |
| 90 | Batch4 | NV-ES-SS09-CONVO21 | SPK05 | 3.114 | 3.813 | 2.704 | 4.85 | \-5.03 | accept | — |
| 91 | Batch5 | NV-FR-SS24-CONVO29 | SPK02 | 3.115 | 3.749 | 2.703 | 3.26 | 0.00 | accept | — |
| 92 | Batch4 | NV-JA-SS07-CONVO21 | SPK03 | 3.117 | 3.707 | 2.716 | 3.50 | \-2.49 | accept | — |
| 93 | Batch4 | NV-EN-SS09-CONVO22 | SPK04 | 3.119 | 4.018 | 2.898 | 1.97 | \-0.93 | accept | — |
| 94 | Batch3 | NV-ES-SS08-CONVO18 | SPK02 | 3.125 | 3.816 | 2.749 | 5.11 | \-7.18 | accept | — |
| 95 | Batch4 | NV-ES-SS10-CONVO23 | SPK05 | 3.126 | 3.814 | 2.715 | 5.20 | \-7.02 | accept | — |
| 96 | Batch5 | NV-GR-SS13-CONVO22 | SPK04 | 3.130 | 3.763 | 2.700 | 3.35 | \-2.49 | accept | — |
| 97 | Batch4 | NV-RU-SS05-CONVO09 | SPK01 | 3.131 | 3.907 | 2.794 | 7.18 | \-0.68 | accept | — |
| 98 | Batch5 | NV-KO-SS11-CONVO27 | SPK04 | 3.133 | 3.848 | 2.722 | 4.51 | \-6.38 | accept | — |
| 99 | Batch3 | NV-ES-SS08-CONVO19 | SPK04 | 3.133 | 3.952 | 2.832 | 4.19 | \-1.76 | accept | — |
| 100 | Batch2 | NV-AR-SS05-CONVO14 | SPK03 | 3.134 | 3.667 | 2.621 | 6.98 | \-14.31 | accept | — |

---

## **4\. Transcription Quality**

**Score: −1 — Deferred pending amended audio.**

Segment-level ASR was run on annotated single-channel turns using Qwen3-ASR-1.7B and Whisper-large-v3. References and hypotheses were normalized with Whisper `BasicTextNormalizer` plus language-specific filler removal. WCMR is the percentage of scored segments whose reference and hypothesis word counts differ.

### **4.1 Current diagnostic ASR results**

| Delivery | ASR model | Scored segments | WER | CER | WCMR |
| :---- | :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| Batch 4 | Qwen3-ASR-1.7B | 31,279 | 12.92% | 6.95% | 25.49% |
| Batch 4 | Whisper-large-v3 | 31,279 | 21.92% | 14.27% | 37.01% |
| Batch 5 | Qwen3-ASR-1.7B | 39,013 | 13.41% | 6.89% | 26.32% |
| Batch 5 | Whisper-large-v3 | 39,013 | 22.14% | 13.98% | 37.08% |

These values remain useful as a baseline, but they combine acceptable and defective audio. They are not the final transcription acceptance result for an amended delivery.

### **4.2 ASR accuracy on rejected channels**

The 13 rejected Batch 4/5 channels were matched to their exact per-channel ASR records. Each rejected channel was compared with all non-rejected Batch 4/5 channels of the same language. The results below are channel-level macro averages, which control for the very different baseline error rates across languages. Lower WER, CER, and WCMR are better.

| ASR model | Rejected WER | Language-matched control WER | WER increase | Relative WER increase | Accuracy drop (`100−WER`) | CER increase | WCMR increase |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| Qwen3-ASR-1.7B | 13.69% | 9.77% | **\+3.92 pp** | \+40.1% | **−3.92 pp** | \+3.43 pp | \+1.57 pp |
| Whisper-large-v3 | 22.61% | 15.75% | **\+6.86 pp** | \+43.6% | **−6.86 pp** | \+5.81 pp | \+3.88 pp |

For both ASR systems, **10 of 13 rejected channels** have worse WER than their same-language controls. The median language-adjusted degradation is \+2.83 percentage points for Qwen3 and \+3.24 points for Whisper.

| Language | Rejected | Controls | Qwen WER: rejected vs control | Qwen difference | Whisper WER: rejected vs control | Whisper difference |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: | ----: |
| German | 3 | 39 | 19.85% vs 8.83% | **\+11.02 pp** | 31.36% vs 15.06% | **\+16.30 pp** |
| Italian | 2 | 57 | 14.35% vs 10.08% | **\+4.27 pp** | 29.60% vs 17.45% | **\+12.15 pp** |
| Russian | 3 | 49 | 15.83% vs 12.98% | **\+2.84 pp** | 18.94% vs 15.63% | **\+3.31 pp** |
| English | 4 | 58 | 9.19% vs 7.81% | **\+1.38 pp** | 15.76% vs 13.60% | **\+2.16 pp** |
| Korean | 1 | 64 | 5.45% vs 10.16% | **\-4.72 pp** | 20.79% vs 23.33% | **\-2.53 pp** |

The largest losses occur in German and Italian. `Batch4/NV-GR-SS08-CONVO15/SPK05` is \+22.68 WER points on Qwen3 and \+37.03 points on Whisper relative to German controls; `Batch4/NV-GR-SS08-CONVO16/SPK04` is \+14.38 and \+18.65 points; and `Batch4/NV-IT-SS10-CONVO27/SPK05` is \+5.24 and \+18.07 points. This supports the conclusion that the confirmed audio artifacts materially reduce recognition accuracy.

The relationship is not one-to-one: three rejected channels perform better than their language baseline on both ASR systems. A channel can remain intelligible to ASR while still being unacceptable for a high-quality speech corpus. ASR error rates therefore complement, but do not replace, listening review, DNSMOS, clipping checks, and bandwidth analysis.

### **4.3 Transcription decision**

Do not issue a final transcription-quality acceptance decision from the current waveforms. Once clean source replacements are delivered, rerun the same segment-level Qwen3 and Whisper evaluation and compare the amended channels against both their current results and same-language controls. The transcription score remains **−1 (deferred)** until that re-evaluation is complete.

---

## **5\. Final Disposition**

| Dimension | Score | Status |
| :---- | :---: | :---- |
| Audio Quality | **1** | Reject as delivered; source amendment required |
| Conversation Structure Quality | **5** | Accept as-is |
| Transcription Quality | **−1** | Deferred until amended audio is re-evaluated |
| **Overall** | **1** | **Reject / amendment required** |

The Batch 4/5 delivery is **not acceptance-ready**. Conversation structure passes, but the following 13 speaker channels and their associated sessions require clean replacement audio or exclusion.

### **5.1 Final rejected samples**

| DNSMOS rank | Batch | Session | Channel | SIG | Final rejection reason |
| ----: | :---- | :---- | :---- | ----: | :---- |
| 1 | Batch4 | NV-GR-SS08-CONVO15 | SPK05 | 2.707 | Severe noise-gating artifact with muffled speech. |
| 4 | Batch4 | NV-EN-SS12-CONVO30 | SPK05 | 2.743 | Severe noise-gating artifact with muffled speech. |
| 6 | Batch4 | NV-GR-SS08-CONVO16 | SPK04 | 2.797 | Audible clipping at a rejection-level severity. |
| 8 | Batch5 | NV-GR-SS09-CONVO17 | SPK07 | 2.827 | Effective bandwidth is limited to approximately 8 kHz. |
| 10 | Batch5 | NV-IT-SS12-CONVO30 | SPK04 | 2.846 | Effective bandwidth is limited to approximately 8 kHz. |
| 20 | Batch4 | NV-RU-SS03-CONVO16 | SPK06 | 2.911 | Severe codec degradation and muffling, with an effective cutoff near 7 kHz. |
| 32 | Batch5 | NV-EN-SS15-CONVO38 | SPK01 | 2.961 | Severe codec or noise-gating artifact. |
| 41 | Batch5 | NV-RU-SS04-CONVO23 | SPK06 | 2.997 | Severely muffled speech with an effective bandwidth near 8 kHz. |
| 43 | Batch4 | NV-EN-SS09-CONVO22 | SPK02 | 3.005 | Severe artifact-related muffling. |
| 44 | Batch4 | NV-RU-SS04-CONVO22 | SPK06 | 3.005 | Severely muffled speech with an effective bandwidth near 8 kHz. |
| 45 | Batch5 | NV-KO-SS11-CONVO26 | SPK04 | 3.006 | Frequent popping artifacts on the speaker channel. |
| 48 | Batch4 | NV-EN-SS12-CONVO31 | SPK05 | 3.019 | Severely muffled speech with an effective bandwidth near 8 kHz. |
| 52 | Batch4 | NV-IT-SS10-CONVO27 | SPK05 | 3.029 | Severely muffled speech with an effective bandwidth near 8 kHz. |

- Reverberation, background noise, clipping, muffling, and post-processing artifacts can all reduce the DNSMOS SIG score. Speech-enhancement processing was applied to the rejected samples, but it did not restore them to acceptable quality.  
    
- This indicates that severe muffling, codec or post-processing artifacts, and clipping cannot be reliably corrected through downstream enhancement and must instead be addressed at the source-acquisition stage.  
    
- Each rejected channel belongs to a different session; therefore, the in-scope exclusion list contains 13 sessions. The corpus-wide Worst-100 appendix also identifies two rejected channels from Batches 2 and 3, but those earlier deliveries fall outside the scope of this report.

**Bad Example \- Bandwidth limited example:**   
Batch4\_NV-RU-SS03-CONVO16\_SPK06  
![][image1]

**Good Example \- Filled up to 16KHz:**   
Batch5\_NV-ES-SS12-CONVO27\_SPK02  
![][image2]  
RFQ \- mentions audio quality explicitly.  
![][image3]

### "Sample rate 44.1kHz"

### "Vendors are responsible for ensuring that each participant’s audio channel is

### configured correctly so that the captured signal is not muffled, filtered, or otherwise altered. If conferencing software is used, vendors must rigorously verify that all audio enhancement and noise reduction features are bypassed or disabled."

### **This sample rate does not mean just saving audio files in 44.1kHz sample rate files. It should include the actual high frequency audio content.** 

### **5.2 Recommended amendments**

- If unprocessed source recordings with genuine spectral content extending through at least 16 kHz are available for the rejected channels, submit those originals as replacements. The amended channels and their synchronized session metadata can then be reviewed again.  
- If suitable clean source recordings are not available, exclude each rejected channel **and its entire associated session** from the dataset rather than retaining a known defective sample.  
- Add open-source DNSMOS P.835 screening to the acquisition and delivery pipeline, with **SIG \> 3.0** as a minimum warning threshold. DNSMOS must not be the sole acceptance metric; combine it with listening review, clipping detection, effective-bandwidth analysis, and other signal-quality checks.  
- **NV-EN-SS16-CONVO39\_mixed.wav** has added silence in the beginning. This does not in-sync with the annotation timestamp. Plus, this file makes audio clipping. Please re-check all the mixed files.  
- Speech-enhancement systems may reduce reverberation and stationary background noise, and bandwidth-extension models may improve some genuinely low-bandwidth recordings. They cannot reliably reconstruct information destroyed by clipping or reverse severe muffling introduced by codecs, noise gates, or post-processing. Clipping and artifact-related muffling must therefore be prevented or corrected at the source-acquisition stage.
